FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data release 5.0 (U.S. cohort, 21 sites, baseline ages 9-11); confirmed in Methods: "The neuroimaging and behavioral data used in this study were obtained from the ABCD Study (data release 5.0)"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis of ABCD data using quadratic mixed-effect models to examine DM usage effects on brain structures over 4 years

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Scientific Reports (2024); no indication of duplicate

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports separate β coefficients for social media usage (h/day) as IV against cerebellum volume (neural outcome): social media usage x Time β=0.02 (p<0.001) and social media usage x Time² β=-0.02 (p<0.001). Named SM variable ("using social media") paired with a neural individual-trait variable (cerebellum volume).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple β coefficients reported for social media usage against brain outcomes (Table 2); e.g., SM usage x Time² on cerebellum: β=-0.02, p<0.001

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative inferential results reported throughout; β coefficients with SEs and p-values in Table 2 for SM-brain relationships

FT_EC7_reason: SM (social media usage) appears as IV in all analyses with brain structures as DV; longitudinal design with SM as predictor means EC7 condition (b) is not met
notes: Paper analyzes three DM types separately: social media, video games, TV/videos. Social media usage is a named SM variable with its own β estimates against neural outcomes. Design is longitudinal (T0 to T4 over 4 years).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data; Methods state: "The Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study is a multi-center longitudinal cohort study that enrolled 11,875 children aged between nine and 10 at baseline from 21 recruitment sites across the US"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data using multivariable logistic regression models examining family environment predictors and subsequent cyberbullying victimization

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Academic Pediatrics (2024); no indication of duplicate

FT_IC2.evidence: The paper measures cyberbullying victimization as the DV ("Have you ever been cyberbullied... online, in texts, or on social media?") and parental monitoring/family conflict as IVs. No phone/SM use variable (time on SM, time on phone, named platform use, texting frequency, app use, etc.) appears as IV or DV with its own coefficient anywhere in the paper. Per binding clarification, cyberbullying alone does not qualify as a smartphone/SM variable; a separately-measured phone/SM variable with its own coefficient is required but absent.

notes: The cyberbullying question references online/texts/social media as the medium but the measured variable is victimization status, not SM use. No SM use variable (IV or DV) with its own coefficient appears anywhere in the paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data; Methods state: "The present study utilized data from the first five waves of the ABCD study. The ABCD comprised the responses of 11,880 youth aged 9 to 10 years at baseline and surveyed annually across 21 study sites in the United States"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal SEM analysis of ABCD data examining traumatic stress exposure, deviant peer association, and dual systems model development (impulse control, sensation-seeking)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Crime & Delinquency (2024); no indication of duplicate

FT_IC2.evidence: No smartphone or social media variable appears anywhere in the paper. IVs are traumatic stress exposure (PTSD subscale of K-SADS) and deviant peer association (Peer Behavior Profile); DVs are impulse control and sensation-seeking (UPPS scale). No phone time, SM time, named platform, texting, app use, or any qualifying SM/phone variable is measured or analyzed. Step 1 and Step 2 both fail.

notes: Paper is entirely about traumatic stress, deviant peers, impulse control, and sensation-seeking. Social media is mentioned once in passing in the introduction (citing peer reactions on social media) but is not measured or analyzed.
